content_markdown: Two ideal gases X and Y are at the same temperature. The mass of a particle of gas X is larger than the mass of a particle of gas Y. Which is correct about the average kinetic energy and the average speed of the particles in gases X and Y?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A, B, C, D) with two columns: 'Average kinetic energy' and 'Average speed'.

options.label: A
options.text: Average kinetic energy: larger for Y; Average speed: larger for Y

options.label: B
options.text: Average kinetic energy: same; Average speed: larger for Y

options.label: C
options.text: Average kinetic energy: same; Average speed: same

options.label: D
options.text: Average kinetic energy: larger for Y; Average speed: same

vibe_explanation: Temperature is a direct measure of the average random kinetic energy of the particles in an ideal gas ($E_k = \frac{3}{2}k_BT$). Since both gases are at the same temperature, their average kinetic energies must be the same. Kinetic energy is also defined as $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. If the kinetic energies are equal but gas X has a larger mass ($m_X > m_Y$), then gas Y must have a larger average speed ($v_Y > v_X$) to compensate.
